metadata.summary: Use typed enums (Literal in Pydantic, enum in JSON Schema) for finite answer sets so constrained decoders eliminate hallucinated labels at sampling time.

content.title: Enum Constraints for Closed Vocabularies
content.summary: Whenever the answer set is finite, declare it as a typed enum (Literal[...] in Pydantic, enum in JSON Schema) instead of a free-form string. Constrained decoders mask every non-enum token to probability zero, so a hallucinated label becomes mathematically impossible. The rule is a one-line schema change with the highest accuracy-per-token ratio of any structured-output trick.

why.p: LLMs default to producing the most likely token among the entire vocabulary. When the field truly has 5 valid values, 99.99% of vocabulary entries are wrong by construction, yet still receive non-zero probability mass and occasionally surface (mostly in long contexts, near token-budget exhaustion, or with smaller models). Strict-mode + enum decoding removes those tokens from the candidate set during sampling. Empirically, ohmeow (2024) and the vLLM Structured Outputs docs both show category-prediction tasks going from 80–95% raw to ~100% with Literal[...] + strict mode. The sampling-time mask, not the prompt, is what closes the gap.

list.item: Any classification field — sentiment, intent, severity, routing label, language code, status state.
Pick-one-of-N action selection inside an agent loop.
Field that maps to a downstream switch/case or DB enum column.
Pipelines where an unknown label silently breaks the next stage.
Open vocabularies (free-text tags, novel entity types, user names) — enum would clip valid answers.
Semi-open sets — use Literal[...] | str plus a discriminated union or post-validation instead.
JSON-mode-only fallback paths on mini/nano models — enums are advisory there, pair with strict mode or a constrained backend (XGrammar, Outlines) to actually enforce them.

section.name: Enum constraints for closed vocabularies
section.p: If a field's valid values are known and finite, encode them as a typed enum, not a free string. Pair the enum with strict structured-output mode so the constraint is enforced at sampling time, not by post-hoc validation.

rule.statement: Every classification or routing field with a finite value set must be declared as Literal[...] (Python) or enum (JSON Schema) AND served under strict mode or a constrained decoder (XGrammar, Outlines, vLLM guided generation).
rule.rationale: Strict-mode constrained decoders mask all non-enum tokens to probability zero, eliminating hallucinated labels. Free-string fields keep the full vocabulary live, so smaller models occasionally emit synonyms or novel labels that break downstream switch/case code.

example.code:
from typing import Literal from pydantic import BaseModel
class Ticket(BaseModel): category: Literal["billing", "tech_support", "refund", "spam", "other"] priority: Literal["P0", "P1", "P2", "P3"] sentiment: Literal["angry", "frustrated", "neutral", "happy"]
example.note: Each Literal compiles to a JSON Schema enum. Under OpenAI strict mode the decoder cannot produce any other token sequence in those slots.
The valid set lives only in a docstring or description. The decoder is free to emit "support", "high priority", "Spam!", and downstream code crashes.

antipattern.description: Listing the valid values inside the prompt body (or Field description) but keeping the field type as str.
antipattern.reason: Soft constraints. The model usually obeys but drifts under long context, low temperature variation, or small models — and a single wrong label silently breaks the next pipeline stage. Enums move the constraint into the decoder mask, where it cannot drift.

section.name: Edge cases: semi-open sets, mini models, language labels
section.p: Some answer sets are mostly closed but allow rare novel values. Forcing a strict enum would silently clip those answers; allowing a free string loses the guarantee for the common case. The pattern is a tagged union with one closed branch and one fallback.

rule.statement: For semi-open sets, model the field as a discriminated union: a Literal[...] branch for known values plus a typed "other" branch carrying the free-text candidate.
rule.rationale: The discriminator forces the decoder to commit to "known" or "other" first, then constrains the rest of the structure. Downstream code can route on the discriminator without parsing free strings.

example.note: Common path stays strict. Novel labels go through a controlled escape hatch with a known shape.

section.p: If strict mode is unavailable (legacy provider, OSS model without grammar support), the enum is advisory only. The model will mostly comply but is free to emit synonyms.

rule.statement: When strict mode is not available, validate the parsed object against the Pydantic Literal AFTER the call and either retry-with-feedback or downgrade the offending field to its closest enum value via a deterministic mapping table.
rule.rationale: Without sampling-time masking, accuracy depends on prompt and luck. A retry loop with explicit "you returned X; valid values are A/B/C" feedback recovers most failures within one extra call; a mapping table absorbs the rest.

antipattern.description: Trusting JSON-mode output to respect an enum without post-validation.
antipattern.reason: Mini/nano models hallucinate enum values under JSON-mode-only fallback. Reported on OpenAI Community threads — the schema is shown to the model but never enforced by the decoder. Production systems must either upgrade to strict mode or validate-and-retry.

template.purpose: Pydantic model with Literal enum fields wired for OpenAI strict mode.

see-also.link: ohmeow — Structuring Enums for Flawless LLM results with Instructor.
Outlines — constrained generation for OSS models when provider strict mode is missing.
